flashcards.front: What is an entity in ER modeling?
flashcards.back: A real-world object/thing with independent existence that can be uniquely identified. Examples: Student, Course, Department.
An entity that depends on another entity for existence and has a partial key. Requires total participation in identifying relationship.
Attribute that can be divided into smaller sub-parts. Example: Address → Street, City, Pincode.
One entity instance associates with multiple instances of another entity. Example: 1 Department has Many Students.
Attribute derivable from other attributes. Example: Age from DateOfBirth. Shown with dashed oval.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of a ternary relationship?
flashcards.back: Simultaneously links three entity types. Example: Doctor-Patient-Medicine prescription relationship.
When all entity instances must participate in a relationship. Shown with double line in ER diagrams.
Attribute that uniquely identifies entity instances. Example: StudentID for Student entity.

flashcards.front: What is role labeling in relationships?
flashcards.back: Used when same entity participates multiple times in relationship. Example: Employee as Manager and Subordinate.

flashcards.front: What is aggregation in ER modeling?
flashcards.back: Treats relationship as higher-level entity to participate in other relationships. Shown with diamond inside diamond.
Attribute with multiple values for single entity. Example: PhoneNumbers. Shown with double oval.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between ER model and relational model?
flashcards.back: ER is conceptual design tool, relational model implements database structure using tables/relations.
